answer: Common scams in Leh Ladakh include taxi overcharging above government fixed rates, fake pashmina and gemstone sales, unlicensed tour operators, and inflated prices at restaurants in tourist-heavy areas. Leh is generally safe for solo travellers.

results.title: Leh Ladakh Travel Guide: Scams, Safety and What to Watch For
results.content: Leh is generally a safe destination, but a few things to watch out for: (1) Taxi Overcharging — Taxis from the airport to town should cost around ₹500–600 on the government fixed-rate card. Always confirm the rate card before entering. Drivers sometimes claim the card doesn't apply after dark or on holidays. (2) Fake Pashmina — Genuine pashmina from a Changpa herder costs ₹3,000–6,000 for a shawl. Anything under ₹800 claiming to be pashmina is acrylic. Ask for a burn test (real pashmina smells like hair, not plastic). (3) Tour operator scams — Unlicensed operators book jeep safaris to Pangong or Nubra but hand over to third parties. Book only with J&K government-registered operators who display their licence number. (4) Altitude sickness 'medicine' — Some pharmacies sell overpriced generic acetazolamide (Diamox) or fake herbal cures. Buy Diamox from a hospital pharmacy only.

results.title: Is Leh Ladakh Safe? Honest Safety Guide for 2026
results.content: Ladakh is one of India's safest destinations for both solo men and women. Crime against tourists is extremely rare. The key risks are: overcharging in unmetered taxis, altitude sickness, and the very real danger of fake gemstone vendors in the Main Bazaar selling 'Ladakhi turquoise' which is dyed glass or plastic. Real turquoise from Changthang herders has an uneven texture and costs more. If a gem looks perfect and uniformly coloured, it's almost certainly fake. Photography restrictions near military areas are strictly enforced — always check before pointing your camera.

results.title: Money and Prices in Leh Ladakh — What Things Actually Cost
results.content: ATMs in Leh are limited and frequently out of cash. There are only 5–6 ATMs in Leh town and they run dry in peak season (July–September). Carry enough cash from Delhi. Credit cards are accepted at very few places. Restaurant overcharging is a minor issue in Fort Road — some places add a 'tourist price' not on the menu. Ask to see the official menu and pay only menu prices. Government-set taxi fares are displayed at the prepaid taxi booths at Kushok Bakula Airport — insist on the printed rate card.

results.title: Leh Ladakh 2026 Travel Tips — Emergency Contacts and Safety
results.content: Tourist Police helpline: 01982-252011. Ambulance: 102. District Hospital Leh: 01982-252012. Police Control Room: 100. The Ladakh Tourism Authority advises all visitors to register at the local police station for treks beyond 10km. Always hire a licensed guide for high-altitude treks. Carry Inner Line Permits for Pangong Tso, Nubra Valley, Tso Moriri, and Dah-Hanu areas — these can be obtained at DC Office Leh.
